summary: Jeff Atwood expresses his long-standing dislike of XSLT, finding validation in Martin Fowler's similar conclusion. While Atwood has no issues with XML as a data format, he argues that XSLT's promise of transforming XML into 'anything' rarely delivers in practice. Fowler's experience moving from XSLT to Ruby for XML transformations reinforced Atwood's belief that procedural code is often simpler and easier than XSLT. The post questions whether XSLT's fundamental premise is realistic or just marketing hype.
key_points: XML is fine for hierarchical data storage, but XSLT as a companion technology is problematic
Martin Fowler, despite being proficient at XSLT, found Ruby significantly easier for XML transformations
Procedural code is often simpler and more practical than XSLT for transformation tasks
The promise of XSLT transforming XML into 'anything' may be fundamentally unrealistic
money_quotes: While useful in theory – 'using a simple XSLT transform, XML can be converted into anything!' – in practice, it takes painful contortions to do anything practical.
I concluded that it was easier and simpler to use procedural code to do the same thing.
I think this may raise some real questions about XSLT. There's still much I like about the power of XSLT, but I hate the syntax and the walls you keep running into.
Maybe the idea of XSLT transforming XML into 'anything' is fundamentally unrealistic – just more Write Once, Run Anywhere snake oil.
key_insight: Technology that promises universal flexibility often fails in practice; sometimes straightforward procedural code beats specialized transformation languages.
